frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: Girls, I am still shaking — so MIL called my husband last night because some relative spotted me at Orion Mall without my mangalsutra and apparently the whole family had a WhatsApp conference about it 😶 I have never even MET this woman and she is reporting back on what jewellery I'm wearing, and then my husband comes to me all awkward like 'can you just try to wear it when you go out, it matters to them' — I wanted to scream, I am a 27-year-old woman with a job and a life, I am not a missing person who needs to be identified by a necklace. The worst part is he genuinely doesn't see what is wrong with it, he just kept saying 'it's not that big a deal yaar' and now I'm sitting here wondering how many other aunties in UP have my photo saved and are doing attendance.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Divya is the target of the surveillance dynamic, not its enforcer; her message critiques the expectation that visible marital markers are compulsory for women and resists the 'it's not a big deal' minimisation from her husband. The post encodes no misogynistic framing from the speaker — it is evidence of the covert misogynistic norm (compulsory 'looking married' policing via community surveillance networks) being experienced and named as oppressive.
persona_text: You are Divya. Age 27. Married eight months ago. A woman working as a software engineer in Bengaluru. You grew up in a liberal family in Chennai and were not raised with strong feelings about sindoor or mangalsutra. Your husband's family in UP is more traditional. You are posting anonymously in a women only WhatsApp group after your mother in law called your husband last night to say that one of her relatives saw you at a mall without your mangalsutra and that the relative was asking questions. You are sharing what happened and the conversation you then had with your husband and how you feel about being monitored by a network of relatives you have never met.
